variables.content: For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

For the ideal (strictly two-dimensional, zero-width, disorder-free) paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas, the extrapolated thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass remains close to unity across the studied density parameters r_s=1, 5, and 10, and the high-precision DMC extrapolations reported here show no evidence for a strong enhancement of m^* at low density in this ideal system.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolation of finite-N DMC quartic-fit effective masses yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.70(3) a.u., where m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} for the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.

Let an experimental $B$--$T$ phase diagram display a 3D melting line extrapolated to higher fields and a 2D full-sample melting curve $T_{2D,full}^{m}(B)$. The proposition claims: locating their intercept (the field at which an extrapolated 3D melting line crosses the $T_{2D,full}^{m}(B)$ curve) provides an experimentally accurate estimate of the finite-size crossover field $B_{cr}$ such that inserting this $B_{cr}$ into the finite-size formula above yields a meaningful estimate of the anisotropy $\gamma$ to within a factor of order unity, despite uncertainties in extrapolation and finite-data sampling.

For the homogeneous unpolarized two-dimensional electron gas in a finite square cell, adding one electron with momentum \mathbf{p} (|\mathbf{p}|>k_F) to the N-particle ground state produces a finite-size correction to the total kinetic-energy contribution of the excitation given by
      \delta T_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}=\frac{\hbar^{2}\mathbf{p}^{2}}{2m}\;Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N}\big[e^{-\Delta_{N}}-1\big],
      where Z_{\mathbf{p}}^{N} is the finite-N quasiparticle weight at momentum \mathbf{p} and \Delta_{N} is the missing-small-q integral defined in conclusion 1. Because \Delta_{N}\sim N^{-1/4} for large N, the leading finite-size error in the near-k_F excitation dispersion used to extract the single-particle effective mass m^{*} (via the linearized relation \varepsilon_{\mathbf{k}}\approx\hbar^{2}k_F(k-k_F)/m^{*}) also scales as N^{-1/4}; therefore, extrapolations that assume faster vanishing finite-size errors can give qualitatively incorrect conclusions about the sign and magnitude of m^{*}-m for typical accessible system sizes.

The quasiparticle effective-mass ratio m^*/m at the Fermi surface, defined from the slope of the quasiparticle energy E(p) at p=p_F by 1/m^*=(1/p)\,dE(p)/dp|_{p=p_F} with E(p)=\epsilon_p+\mathrm{Re}\,\Sigma(p,E(p)) and \epsilon_p=\hbar^2p^2/(2m), is found in numerical evaluations for the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas to be substantially reduced by the inclusion of short-range vertex corrections embodied in the local-field factors G(q) and C(q); the high-density asymptotic expansion gives
      $$
      m^*/m=\left\{1-\frac{\alpha r_s}{2\pi}\left(2+\ln\frac{\alpha r_s}{\pi}\right)+\cdots\right\}^{-1},
      $$
      with \alpha=(4/9\pi)^{1/3}, which predicts m^*/m<1 in the high-density limit, while RPA results exceed unity for r_s\ge 2.23 and the Rice approximation results exceed unity for r_s\ge 2.7; by contrast, the present approximation that retains both particle-hole and particle-particle local-field corrections yields m^*/m values that remain below unity throughout the metallic-density region examined and cross above unity only at r_s\simeq 5.5.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=1, extrapolating finite-N quasiparticle effective masses m^*(N) produced by quartic fits to DMC energy bands and fitting m^*(N)=m^*(∞)+b N^{-γ} yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=0.955(2) in hartree atomic units (a.u.), where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the Fermi wave vector of the infinite system.

With the discharge coefficient defined as
      $$
      C_d=\frac{\dot{m}}{\dot{m}_{th}},
      $$
      where $\dot m$ is the actual mass flow measured or computed and $\dot m_{th}$ is the theoretical choked-flow mass flow used as reference, the ideal-gas CFD computations (both numerical and classical inviscid theory) show $C_d$ increasing with Reynolds number and approaching unity. When the real-gas CFD solution for hydrogen is reduced using the inviscid ideal-gas theoretical choked-flow rate $\dot m_{th}$ given above, the resulting $C_d$ increases and can exceed unity. Defining instead a real-gas-based theoretical mass flow by using cross-sectional mean throat properties,
      $$
      \dot{m}_{th}^{\mathrm{(real)}}=A\,\bar{\rho}_{th}\,\bar{u}_{th},
      $$
      where $A$ is throat area and $\bar{\rho}_{th}$ and $\bar{u}_{th}$ are the cross-sectionally averaged density and axial velocity at the throat from the real-gas flow field, produces a corrected discharge coefficient $C_d=\dot m/\dot m_{th}^{\mathrm{(real)}}$ that remains below unity; in the present computations this corrected $C_d$ exhibits a maximum below unity at a throat Reynolds number near $Re_{th}=0.6\times10^{7}$. Experimental discharge-coefficient data reported in the literature for high-pressure hydrogen, which were reduced using the ideal-gas theoretical mass flow $\dot m_{th}$, show a maximum $C_d$ exceeding unity and differ substantially from the present CFD results reduced using a real-gas theoretical reference.

For finite-system variational Monte Carlo studies of the two-dimensional electron gas in the density range $1\le r_{s}\le5$ and system sizes tested ($N=26,58,98$), extracting the effective mass $m^{*}$ by (i) computing correlated energy differences among excited states at fixed particle number and (ii) fitting those differences to the angular expansion for $\Delta E_{\alpha\beta}$ to obtain $f_{1}^{s}$ and then $m^{*}$ via $m^{*}/m=(1-\tfrac{1}{4}r_{s}^{2}N f_{1}^{s})^{-1}$ produces results with much smaller and more systematic finite-size dependence than extracting $m^{*}$ from energy differences between ground and excited states combined with external compressibility and susceptibility inputs; this conclusion is supported by the observed near-independence of the fitted combinations $N f_{l}$ across the tested $N$ values when the excited-state-difference method is used.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=10, extrapolating finite-N DMC effective masses obtained from quartic-band fits gives the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.03(4) a.u., where m^* is defined by m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.
